critic_raw:
1. THE 15% IMPROVEMENT THRESHOLD IS DEFINED IN ACCURACY, WHICH IS INSENSITIVE TO CHURN SIGNAL. The hypothesis claims 'at least 15% improvement in churn prediction accuracy.' Churn in a streaming service is typically 3-8% monthly. A model predicting no churn achieves 92-97% accuracy. A 15% improvement over this floor (e.g., from 94% to ~94%+) is trivially achieved by learning any marginal signal, while the actual predictive improvement is negligible. The hypothesis is operationalized in a metric that makes the threshold meaninglessly easy to hit. AUROC is chosen as the actual evaluation metric but is not the metric in the hypothesis threshold — this creates a disconnect between what the hypothesis claims and what the experiment measures.

2. SENTIMENT ANALYSIS FEATURES ARE TEMPORALLY COUPLED TO CHURN. Customer reviews are written after a service interaction — often just before cancellation. If the sentiment feature is extracted from a review written in the same month as the churn event, it encodes the decision already made. The design should enforce a temporal gap: sentiment features must be from at least one billing cycle before the churn label. The design acknowledges that 'temporal autocorrelation might exist' and dismisses it, but this is not autocorrelation — it is prospective label leakage from review-writing behavior that precedes cancellation.

3. GRADIENT BOOSTING BASELINE RATHER THAN MATCHED MODEL TYPE. The design uses gradient boosting as the experimental model (with sentiment) and logistic regression as the baseline (transactional only). This confounds model architecture with feature set in both directions simultaneously: the experimental model is more powerful AND has more informative features. A proper test requires: (a) logistic regression with transactional features vs. gradient boosting with transactional features — to measure model architecture effect; and (b) gradient boosting with transactional only vs. gradient boosting with transactional + sentiment — to measure feature contribution. The current design cannot attribute the difference to sentiment features.

4. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT FOR USER SUBSCRIPTION EVENTS CONFLATES DIFFERENT USERS' TEMPORAL POSITIONS. Users acquired in month 1 of the 3-year period have 36 months of behavioral history; users acquired in month 33 have 3 months of history. A random split will include both long-tenure and short-tenure users in training and test. The model trained on long-tenure users will not generalize to predicting churn for newly acquired users — a critical operational use case. The design does not stratify by tenure cohort, meaning the reported performance is an average over a highly heterogeneous population.
all_issues_raised: 15% accuracy improvement threshold is measured in the wrong metric; accuracy at high base rates makes the threshold trivially achievable
Sentiment features from reviews written before cancellation constitute prospective label leakage, not just temporal autocorrelation
Experimental model (gradient boosting + sentiment) vs. baseline (logistic regression + transactional) confounds architecture and feature set simultaneously
No stratification by user tenure cohort; model performance averaged over heterogeneous populations does not predict new-user churn
Quantified 15% improvement claim is not operationalized: percentage point vs. relative improvement undefined; accuracy and AUROC can disagree on whether the threshold is met
Temporal offset between sentiment feature extraction and churn label unspecified: contemporaneous sentiment is not a predictive feature
Comparison conflates model class and feature set: 2x2 design required to isolate sentiment contribution from gradient boosting advantage
Vocabulary drift: TF-IDF fitted on training reviews will have OOV coverage gaps for test-set reviews written in later time periods
User-level temporal leakage: monthly records from the same user scattered across train and test sets rather than chronological user-level hold-out
15% improvement threshold is ambiguous — not defined in AUROC units; appears arbitrary
Sentiment features may not be temporally aligned with monthly churn labels; post-churn review leakage possible
User-month level split ignores user-level repeated observations; user-level leakage inflates AUROC
Gradient boosting vs. logistic regression confounds model complexity with sentiment feature inclusion
Temporal independence is assumed without empirical validation; burden of proof is inverted
